g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of Write Policies in cache memory systems. Learn how processors handle data updates efficiently.
A write policy determines how data is handled when the processor writes to memory locations present in cache memory.
g.text: When processor writes to memory, the system decides whether to update:

g.data-narration: The choice between write policies impacts performance, data consistency, power consumption, and system complexity in microcontrollers.
Write-Through updates both cache and main memory simultaneously on every write operation, ensuring data consistency.
Write-Through offers simplicity and data consistency - main memory always has the latest data, making cache coherence easier.

g.text: No data loss if cache fails

g.data-narration: The main drawback is performance overhead - every write requires slow main memory access, consuming high memory bandwidth.

g.text: Slow main memory on every write
High traffic between cache and memory

g.data-narration: Write-Back writes data only to cache initially. Main memory is updated later when the cache block is evicted.
The dirty bit is a status flag tracking whether cache data differs from main memory. It indicates modified data needing write-back.
g.text: Dirty Bit - The Key to Write-Back

g.data-narration: Write-Back reduces memory traffic significantly. Multiple writes to same block need only one main memory write during eviction.
Write-Back has complexity overhead - requires dirty bit tracking, write-back buffers, and more complex coherence protocols.

g.text: Needs dirty bit and write-back logic

g.data-narration: On write miss, systems use either write-allocate (load block then write) or no-write-allocate (write directly to memory).

g.text: Load block into cache → Then write
Write directly to memory, skip cache

g.data-narration: Comparing 1000 writes with 60% hit rate: Write-Through needs 1000 memory writes, Write-Back needs only up to 400.
The efficiency formula shows write-back memory writes equal writes multiplied by miss rate, ideal for high hit rates.

g.text: where w = writes, h = hit rate

g.data-narration: A comprehensive comparison: Write-Through is simpler and consistent, while Write-Back offers better performance and power efficiency.
Remember: dirty bit tracks modifications, write-back defers memory updates, and choose based on application needs for power or consistency.
